g.data-narration: Welcome to Directory Implementation. We'll explore how operating systems organize and manage files efficiently.
A directory is a special file that maps file names to their metadata or data block pointers, enabling efficient file operations.
g.text: A directory is a special file that contains
information about other files - specifically a
mapping from file names to file metadata

g.data-narration: There are two main data structures: Linear Lists (simple but slow) and Hash Tables (fast but complex). Let's explore both!
A linear list stores file names sequentially with pointers to their FCBs. Each entry contains the filename and inode pointer.
g.text: Store directory as a sequential list of file names with pointers to their data blocks.

g.data-narration: Operations on linear lists: Search scans sequentially O(n), Create checks duplicates then adds O(n), Delete searches and removes O(n).
Linear lists are simple to implement with minimal space overhead. No complex data structures needed - just an array!
g.text: Simple to implement - No complex algorithms
Minimal space overhead - Just the list
Perfect for: Small directories with few files

g.data-narration: The main drawback is slow search - O(n) means scanning thousands of entries. Deletion also creates gaps that need handling.
g.text: For directories with 10,000+ files, each operation becomes painfully slow!

g.data-narration: Two improvements: Sorted list enables binary search O(log n), while B-trees provide balanced tree performance for all operations.
g.text: Trade-off: Insert/Delete become O(n) for sorted list. B-Tree balances all operations.

g.data-narration: A hash table uses a hash function to convert filenames into array indices, enabling O(1) average lookup time.
g.text: Use a hash function to convert filename to an index for fast lookup.

g.data-narration: When two files hash to the same index, we use chaining - linked lists at each slot handle collisions elegantly.
g.text: Hash Table: Collision Handling with Chaining
When two filenames hash to the same index, use chaining - linked lists at each slot.

g.data-narration: Hash table operations: Search averages O(1) by hashing and scanning short chain, Create and Delete also average O(1).
Hash tables provide O(1) average search - much faster than linear search! Greatly improves file system performance.
g.text: Best for: Large directories with thousands of files

g.data-narration: Hash tables have fixed size - if directory grows, you may need expensive rehashing. Collisions can also degrade performance.
Compare: Linear list is simple O(n) for all operations. Hash table is faster O(1) average but has collision and fixed-size issues.
g.text: Comparison: Linear List vs Hash Table

g.data-narration: Real file systems use different structures: FAT uses linear lists, ext2 uses linked lists, ext4 uses HTree, NTFS uses B+ trees.
